coredata.dc:title: Na-doped carbon nanodots: shed light on the concentration modulated photoluminescence and two-photon absorption performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Velusamy, Jayaramakrishnan
Ramos-Ortiz, Gabriel
coredata.dc:description: Metal-doped carbon nanodots (m-CNDs) have recently received attention due to their tunable optical properties useful in biophotonics and optoelectronics. Here, we present a green, rapid, and facile synthesis of m-CNDs via a one-pot solid phase (powder) flux technique in a solvent-free environment, with Na as a metal doping agent adsorbed to their surface. The average radius of the m-CNDs in colloids of different concentrations varied from 1.5 up to 5 nm due to aggregation effects. Concentration-dependent optical properties from these colloids were determined by studying thoroughly one- and two-photon excitations. The results show that the Na-doping in m-CNDs not only can enhance 100% the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), but also increases considerably the two-photon absorption cross sections (σTPA) compared with pristine carbon nanodots. m-CNDs exhibited multifarious emissive centers with PLQY from 10% to 22%, while a maximum σTPA of 19.7 × 103 GM was observed for m-CNDs with a radius of 5 nm at 730 nm, a nonlinearity that is comparable or higher than in other heteroatoms (metal and nonmetal) doped carbon nanodots. These large values of σTPA are discussed in the context of previous reports for a variety of carbon nanodots, pointing out that Na had not been used previously as a doping agent to enhance optical nonlinearities in m-CNDs. This work offers a novel avenue for tuning σTPA in m-CNDs and endows them with potential for various applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Two-photon absorption
Aggregation
Photoluminescence

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: G. Ramos-Ortiz acknowledges economical support from CONACyT (Grant 215708 ). We thank Sandeep Surendra Panikar for the assistance in cell viability assay.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Artistic representation of the one-pot strategy by using powder flux technique to fabricate CNDs and m-CNDs under a solvent-free environment. (b) Water colloids of pristine CNDs and m-CNDs under daylight (upper panel) and the UV lamp (lower panel).

(a) Wide-angle powder XRD patterns of pristine CNDs (lower panel) and m-CNDs (upper panel) fabricated by powder flux technique displaying a broad peak centered around 27° (2θ) corresponding to a characteristic band of highly disorder amorphous carbon. (b) EDX spectra show the C, N, O, and Na compositions of pristine CNDs (lower panel) and m-CNDs (upper panel). (c) Raman spectra of pristine CNDs (lower panel) and m-CNDs (top panel) showing a combination of disordered (D band) and ordered (G band) levels of carbon.

Representative TEM images for the (a) prepared pristine CNDs at 0.5 mg mL−1 and m-CNDs at different concentrations (b) 0.5, (c) 1.0 and (d) 1.5 mg mL−1. The inset in (d) displays the FFT where the high frequency is showed. The size distribution histograms and Gaussian fit for the prepared samples are also presented (e-h).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of pristine CNDs at the concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 (1) and different concentrations of m-CNDs as 0.5 (2), 1.0 (3), and 1.5 (4) mg mL−1. (b) Photoluminescence spectra of pristine CNDs and concentration modulated m-CNDs with excitation of 340 nm.

Plots of excitation dependent emission in pristine CNDs and m-CNDs along with color contour maps (insets) for (a) pristine CNDs at concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 and m-CNDs at the concentration of (b) 0.5 mg mL−1, (c) 1.0 mg mL−1 and (d) 0.5 mg mL−1.

(a-c) Two-photon excited fluorescence (TPEF) spectra obtained at different excitation wavelengths (680 −760 nm) and different concentrations of m-CNDs: left panel 0.5 mg mL−1; central panel 1.0 mg mL−1; right panel 1.5 mg mL−1. Emissive centers are marked in blue, green, and orange oval shapes. In these experiments, 100 fs pulses were provided by Ti:Sa laser at the repetition rate of 80 MHz with a typical energy of 1.25 nJ/pulse.
The σTPA values from aqueous colloid of m-CNDs. Inset shows one- and two-photon induced emissions from a m-CNDs colloid.
Two-photon absorption cross sections of various types of carbon nanodots.

head.ce:title: Na-doped carbon nanodots: shed light on the concentration modulated photoluminescence and two-photon absorption performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigaciones en Óptica, A. P. 1-948, 37000 León, Guanajuato, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Investigaciones en Óptica, A. P. 1–948, 37000 León, Gto., México

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-doped carbon nanodots (m-CNDs) have recently received attention due to their tunable optical properties useful in biophotonics and optoelectronics. Here, we present a green, rapid, and facile synthesis of m-CNDs via a one-pot solid phase (powder) flux technique in a solvent-free environment, with Na as a metal doping agent adsorbed to their surface. The average radius of the m-CNDs in colloids of different concentrations varied from 1.5 up to 5 nm due to aggregation effects. Concentration-dependent optical properties from these colloids were determined by studying thoroughly one- and two-photon excitations. The results show that the Na-doping in m-CNDs not only can enhance 100% the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), but also increases considerably the two-photon absorption cross sections (σTPA) compared with pristine carbon nanodots. m-CNDs exhibited multifarious emissive centers with PLQY from 10% to 22%, while a maximum σTPA of 19.7 × 103 GM was observed for m-CNDs with a radius of 5 nm at 730 nm, a nonlinearity that is comparable or higher than in other heteroatoms (metal and nonmetal) doped carbon nanodots. These large values of σTPA are discussed in the context of previous reports for a variety of carbon nanodots, pointing out that Na had not been used previously as a doping agent to enhance optical nonlinearities in m-CNDs. This work offers a novel avenue for tuning σTPA in m-CNDs and endows them with potential for various applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The initial discovery of photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs), achieved unintentionally during a single-wall nanotube purification process, occurred in 2004 [1]. Since then, several characterization studies have been intensively performed to scrutinizing their properties. CNDs were classified as novel amorphous nanoparticles and regarded as a unique transitional state of carbon. In general, CNDs comprise various encouraging characteristics as intense photoluminescence (PL), resistance to photobleaching, chemical inertness, ease of preparation, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [2,3]. CNDs have been employed in emergent technologies, including biological imaging, energy conversion/storage, catalysis, drug delivery, environmental monitoring and remediation. [4–6]. Notably, in applications that require the use of efficient luminescence, CNDs can offer prominent high PLQY as high a 94% [4], multicolor emission and excitation dependent properties [7–11]. When used as luminescent probes in biological imaging, CNDs surpass in many cases the optical properties of traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). All these advantageous properties of CNDs have led to increasing efforts to get insight into the fundamental mechanisms of their PL promoted either by linear (one-photon) and nonlinear (two-photon) absorption [2,3,5,12].

The probability of generating excited electronic states in a molecule or particle by the two-photon absorption (TPA) process is determined by the two-photon absorption cross-section, denoted as σTPA. This parameter is expressed in Goeppert-Mayer units: 1 GM = 10−50 cm4 s/photon. Cao et al. reported for the first time an assessment of the TPA process in luminescent colloids of CNDs, obtaining the appealing high value of σTPA = 39,000 GM under the excitation wavelength of 800 nm [13]. Similar high values (on average 32,000 GM at 800 nm) in CNDs were reported by Kong et al. when they prepared inorganic-organic probes for two-photon biosensing and pH screening in living cells and tissues [14]. J. Chen’s group demonstrated CNDs conjugated with a porphyrinoid photosensitizer (TMPyP). Such CNDs were employed in two-photon photodynamic therapy implemented through a very efficient (45%) fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. They observed in TMPyP a substantial increase of σTPA from 100 GM to 15,985 GM at 700 nm after conjugation with CNDs [15]. G. Tong et al. reported CNDs (16,000 GM at 808 nm) passivated with a hyperbranched polymer and applied them to cellular fluorescence imaging [16].

Although the literature is not abundant in systematic studies on the factors that enhance the optical nonlinearities in CNDs, it is known that the σTPA is determined by the choice of chemical precursors and the employed protocol of synthesis. For instance, the use of melamine, glycerol, and nitrogen (N) introduced as heteroatom produced N-rich CNDs and enhanced the nonlinearities (σTPA of 1 320 GM) compared with CNDs in absence of N [17]. N-rich CNDs comprising donor-π-acceptor (D-π-A) configurations are of interest because they boost crystallinity and favor rigid planes; the largest σTPA reported for N-rich CNDs with D-π-A configuration is 61,200 GM [3]. In N-rich CNDs, the TPA process is enhanced by the amplification of the intramolecular electron-transfer between N and the cyclic structure of CNDs. On the other hand, by using first-principles calculations it was predicted that the presence of polymer chains of specific D–π–A structure and large conjugated rigid sp2 domains could tailor the nonlinear optical properties (1 174 GM) of CNDs [18]. The optical nonlinearities in CNDs have been also addressed by a controlled chemical cutting of graphene oxide [19] leading to σTPA of 130 GM. Although this nonlinearity resulted relatively small, that work explains the one- and two-photon excitation mechanisms involving isolated sp2 clusters and π-π stacking interactions in adjacent layers of CNDs. Metal-doping is another strategy that promotes intramolecular charge transfer and can also increment the crystalline nature of CNDs and, in turn, enhance the TPA efficiency. CNDs decorated covalently with Ruthenium (Ru) complexes produced 10-fold larger σTPA (1 118 GM at 810 nm) compared to pristine CNDs [20], while Sulfur (S) and Selenium (Se) co-doped CNDs with covalent bonds obtained via the hydrothermal treatment of polythiophene and diphenyl diselenide led to intense TPA (σTPA = 30,045 GM at 880 nm) [21].

The TPA process in some CNDs is significantly more efficient than in most organic molecules [22,23] and results similar to semiconductor QDs [24,25]. Notice that the TPA efficiencies reported for CNDs varies is a wide range (102-104 GM) due to their structural and composition differences and the variety of fabrication process employed, in which metal doping as strategy to boosts nonlinearities deserves further investigations. The two-photon excited fluorescence (TPEF) technique is the comparative method commonly employed to calculate σTPA for CNDs in colloids; in this method the PL upon TPA from the sample and a standard are compared under the same experimental conditions [26]. In the application of TPEF the concentration and PLQY of both CNDs and standard should be correctly determined. However, it was mentioned earlier the strategies such as surface state modification through either heteroatom or metal doping tune not only the TPA efficiencies but also generate multifarious PL properties in CNDs. The multifarious emissions can also be induced from particle size effects, the use of capping agents, surfactants (which sometimes are not entirely removed from the final products) and adhered small emissive molecules to the intrinsic core of CNDs [8,10,11,27]. Likewise, the linear and nonlinear optical properties can also exhibit a concentration dependence.

In view of the above considerations, in this work we seek to compare the optical properties of pristine CNDs versus sodium (Na) doped CNDs (m-CNDs). A wide search in the literature indicates that the screening of possible enhancement of optical nonlinearities in carbon nanodots when Na is used as a metal doping has not received attention, although the adsorption of Na ions into their surface has been demonstrated when NaOH is used as catalyst in the synthesis process [28–30]. TPA and multifarious emissions are the properties considered in these comparative investigations for which colloids of pristine and metal-doped CNDs were prepared at different concentrations. This study provides insights into the achievable σTPA in colloids of Na-doped CNDs. Further, Green synthesis strategy has been contemplated for this work. We demonstrated green and solid phase one-pot powder flux technique for the fabrication of CNDs in a solvent-free environment. The proposed method is highly advantageous in terms of simplicity, reproducibility, and without the use of any capping agent. It is worth to mention that most of the reported CNDs with multicolor emissions are synthesized with hydrothermal and solvothermal methods involving the use of organic solvents, sophisticated equipment, high-temperature, or high-pressure synthesis conditions.

Urea (U), citric acid (CA), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) as starting materials were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Mexico) and used as received. The optimization of the experimental conditions is presented in the Supplementary Information. In this solid-phase CNDs fabrication procedure [31], the precursors U (3 g) and CA (1.5 g) with a 2:1 mass ratio were grounded in an agate mortar. The mix of fine powder was transferred to an Ace pressure tube flask and heated at 180 °C for 1 hour (h) using a high rate of stirring under the solvent-free condition with a securely sealed screw cap ( Fig. 1). A black semi-dry molten was noticeable in the Ace pressure tube (Fig. S1) after 1 h of reaction; then, the flask was cold down to ambient temperature. The final product (CNDs) appearance was a black solid from this first phase of preparation. A similar procedure was used to prepare the m-CNDs, but in this case, after 1 h of flux, 0.1 g of NaOH pellets were added in swift stirring, and then the mixture was kept at 180 °C for 20 additional minutes. The appearance of the original black molten (first phase) becomes brownish after addition NaOH reaction, and the final dried product was a pale orange powder (Fig. S1). After the preparation, non-fluorescent deposits were removed from samples by purification procedures which included successive steps of centrifugation, the use of PTFE membrane filters and dialysis (for more details of how the carbon nanodots were purified see Supplementary Information). The final powder of both CNDs and m-CNDs was dissolved in deionized water for further characterization studies such as powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and Raman spectral analysis. Samples with different concentrations were prepared: 0.5 mg of CNDs and 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 mg of m-CNDs dissolved in 1 mL of deionized water, respectively, for further optical analysis.

The mechanism of CNDs synthesis in solid phase is ascribed to the chemical changes of precursors by the liberation of hydrogen and volatile substances producing concentration of carbon content [32,33]. In particular, citric acid (CA) and urea (U) are carbon, hydrogen and heteroatoms sources, where the dry heating results in the formation of viscous carbonaceous residues, causing likely the rearrangement of the organic molecules and reordering of carbon layers into compact and amorphous nanoparticles. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are no detailed theoretical (thermodynamic or kinetic) or experimental explanations about how carbonization in solid phase produce surface-functionalized carbon nanoparticles [31,34].

In this work, the preliminary formation of pristine CNDs and m-CNDs have been confirmed through direct methods; XRD, EDX, Raman spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) [35]. The XRD patterns depicted in Fig. 2(a) show a broad feature centered at 27° (2θ) corresponding to the (002) reflection (d002 = 3.2 Å), which is smaller in inter-planner spacing than in bulk graphite (3.36 Å); consequently, CNDs and m-CNDs can be considered as sp2 graphitic carbons with stacking faults known as turbostratic carbons or disorder graphite carbon [31,36,37]. In the case of m-CNDs, some additional peaks are present in the pattern assessing the successful addition of metal dopant. EDX elemental composition analysis of pristine CNDs and m-CNDs is presented in Fig. 2(b); detected peaks are assigned to different elements like carbon (0.27 KeV), nitrogen (0.39 KeV), and oxygen (0.52 KeV); additionally, the peak at 1.04 KeV indicates the incorporation of Na metal cations in m-CNDs. It should be noted that the samples under characterization were purified in three steps including a procedure of dialysis. During the dialysis the unreacted materials CA, U and NaOH and other byproducts permeate leaving inside the dialysis bag the carbon nanodots decorated with chemical groups or molecules covalently linked or adsorbed physically to the surface. By using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Zhu J. et al. [28] demonstrated recently that the use of NaOH in the synthesis of carbon nanodots produce Na ions physically adsorbed to the surface. Based in these results we ascribe the presence of Na in dialyzed m-CNDs (see Fig. 2b) precisely to adsorption of Na ions. The post-treatment of pristine CNDs with NaOH led to adsorbed Na ions by carboxylic groups on the surface of the nanodots [28–30]. Raman spectral studies (Fig. 2(c)) reveal the two dominant Raman characteristic peaks (D and G) of low dimensional carbon systems (e.g., graphene, carbon black, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes). The bands with peaks in the range 1320 and 1360 cm–1 and 1520 and 1600 cm−1 are well-defined as D and G-bands, respectively [37,38]. Pristine and metal doped CNDs display the presence of D-band centered around 1349 cm−1, which arises from vibrations of sp3-hybridized carbon atoms with hanging bonds or disordered carbon atoms. Generally, D-band originates from aromatic conjugated sp3 six-membered carbon rings, hence domination in D-band peak intensity in CNDs and m-CNDs reflects large number of such ring clusters. Consequently, broad D-band reflects disorder in aromatic clusters [8,37,39,40]. In-plane stretching vibration motion of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice, corresponding to G-band, are observed at 1530cm- 1 and 1550cm- 1 in pristine CNDs and m-CND, respectively. Moreover, compared to the Raman spectrum of pristine CNDs, the peak position of the D-band in m-CNDs remains unaltered, while G-band has shifted 20 cm−1, which can be ascribed to the phonon stiffening in the prepared m-CNDs [41,42].

TEM micrographs and size distribution histograms obtained from colloids at three different concentrations are shown in Fig. 3(a-h). The sample of pristine CNDs at concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 are spherical and nearly monodispersed, with an average diameter of about 3 nm. The micrograph for m-CNDs at the same concentration shows almost sphere-shaped particles with an average 3.5 nm diameter, which is slightly larger than in pristine CNDs. By increasing the concentration of m-CNDs to 1.0 and 1.5 mg mL−1, micrographs reveal average particle diameters about 7 nm and 10 nm, respectively, with significant size dispersion. The increments of sizes observed by TEM are associated with the aggregation of m-CNDs particles, the effect that is highly modulated by the concentration [43–45]. At a high concentration, several m-CNDs are aggregated together by Van der Waals forces (intermolecular interactions) to form nanoclusters, while large number of –CO and –OH bonds get along, which leads to the higher polarity on the surfaces of nanoclusters. This high polarity of the nanosized clusters may reveal the origins for the excitation-dependent emission behaviors in the m-CNDs [44–47].

The optical properties of aqueous colloids of CNDs were determined by UV-Vis absorption and PL spectroscopies. Pristine CNDs display two absorption bands at 243 nm and 341 nm ( Fig. 4(a)) associated with the electronic transitions S0 → S2 and S0 → S1, respectively. The absorption band located at ~ 243 nm (Band 1 - core state) is denoted as a core state with the highest energy level transition (π–π * ) attributed to sp2 carbogenic domains. The second absorption band centered at 341 nm (Band 2 - edge state) is assigned to n–π * transition arising from the edge of CNDs. The edge state of CNDs has been reported previously in some blue-emitting CNDs, denoting the atoms at the crystalized edge within the carbon core [48,49]. In the case of m-CNDs, an additional band (Band 3 – surface state) appear at 460 nm; Band 3 arises due to the adherence between edge bonded organic functional groups (O, H, N) and surface Na ion dopant.

m-CNDs exhibited different PL peaks compared with pristine CNDs. For instance, by exciting the absorption Band 2 at 340 nm, pristine CNDs (concentration 0.5 mg mL−1) exhibits a PL peak at 414 nm while m-CNDs at the same concentration emits at 443 nm (Fig. 4.(b)) confirming that the metal dopant plays a significant role in emission properties. For colloids of m-CNDs at concentrations of 1.0 and 1.5 mg mL−1 the PL peak appears at 437 and 432 nm, respectively. To explore the dependence of PL on excitation wavelengths, a series of PL spectroscopic measurements were conducted varying the excitation from 300 to 500 nm in steps of 10 nm. The output emission maps of aqueous colloids of CNDs and m-CNDs at different concentrations are presented in Fig. 5(a-d). The overall PL features of the samples are as follows. First, the emissive peak from pristine CNDs (around 417 nm) under excitation in the wavelength range 300–340 nm undergoes continuous increments in intensity. When the excitation wavelength is tuned from 350 to 500 nm, the emission peak is red shifted between 417 nm and 540 nm, with a decrease in intensity. In the case of 0.5 mg mL−1 m-CNDs, the emission peak observed around 443 nm is nearly excitation independent for the excitation in the range 300–360 nm, showing increments of intensity (Fig. 5(b)); however, when the excitation wavelength scans above 370 nm up to 410 nm, the PL is continuously red-shifted, and the spectrum becomes broader while the intensity decreases. By scanning the excitation from 420 to 500 nm that coincides with the absorption Band 3 (surface state), another emissive center appears at 555 nm with continuous red-shifting and decreasing in intensity up to reach 570 nm. Hence, the inclusion of metal ion creates an additional small emissive center corresponding to the excitation of surface states; to the best of our knowledge, this anomalous emission behavior so far had not been reported in any metal-doped CNDs. Higher concentrations of m-CNDs (1.0 and 1.5 mg mL−1) followed similar PL trends, but for the sample with a concentration of 1.5 mg mL−1, the secondary emissive center becomes more intense than the primary emissive center (Fig. 5(d)). Thus, in these samples the intrinsic luminescence from sp2-carbon networks is the origin of the emission at short wavelengths of excitation, being that luminescence nearly excitation-independent up to 340 nm; the high polarity of surface O, N, and sodium ions account for the excitation-dependent PL at a longer wavelength (beyond 360 nm) of excitation. This abrupt change in the PL emissive centers when the concentration of m-CNDs has increased is associated with the aggregation of particles shown by TEM characterization and size augmentation effects. The existence and transformation of two or more PL centers as a function of concentration was also reported by Y. Chen et al. and X. Meng et al. but for the case of metal-free CNDs [11,44]. Maximum PLQY of 10.1% was measured by X. Meng et. al. in their CNDs with a tendency of decreasing as a function of concentration. In our case, the metal doping in CNDs enhanced the PLQY compared with pristine CNDs. The PLQY were determined at 340 nm of excitation by using the integrating sphere method (spectrofluorometer from Edinburgh Intruments, model FS5). At the concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1, the PLQY increased from 10% in pristine CNDs to 22% in m-CNDs. The incorporation of Na metal ions on the surface greatly influences the electronic surface states of m-CNDs. The surface states are generally defined as PL centers formed by the hybridization of the chemical groups and the carbon core [35,50,51] In m-CNDs, the incorporation of Na ions causes the rapid transfer of electrons from the defect states to excited energy levels, which are responsible for the red-shifted emission and enchantment of PL. The PLQY showed concentration dependence, being 15% and 12% for m-CNDs at the concentrations of 1.0 and 1.5 mg mL−1, respectively.

The Na-doping of CNDs in our flux method has improved not only the PLQY, but it also has boosted the nonlinear optical properties. The nonlinear optical properties of m-CNDs were investigated using the TPEF technique [26] with femtosecond (fs) pulsed laser excitation. The nonlinear properties of pristine CNDs were also tested; however, they did not show any detectable TPEF signal even at the laser pumping average power as high as 500 mW (6.25 nJ/pulse). Hypothetically, this enhancement of the optical nonlinearities from CNDS to m-CNDs is due to intramolecular electron-transfer between Na and the cyclic structure. In this work, we seek also to evaluate the concentration effects on the measurements of σTPA. In these TPEF experiments a methanol solution of Rhodamine 6 G (R6G) at the concentration of 1.04 × 10−5 M was used as standard. The σTPA value is calculated from the TPEF using the following equation [26]:
(1)
σ
TPA
=
σ
R
F
s
C
R
PLQY
R
η
R
F
R
C
S
PLQY
S
η
S
where F is the integrated area of the two-photon excited emission spectrum, C is the concentration of the sample and η is the solvent's refractive index. The subscripts S and R denote sample and reference, respectively. The PLQY for R6G (94.3%) was determined experimentally using the integrating sphere method. The values of σ R for R6G at different wavelengths are reported in the literature [52]. To avoid ambiguities in the measurement of σTPA special care must be taken to define the type of concentration to be utilized in Eq. (1). In this work, the concentrations C S and C R were chosen to be the number of nanoparticles/mL for the case of carbon nanodots and the number of molecules/mL for the case of the standard R6G. Notice that this approach circumvents the ambiguities that might arise in the experimental calculation of σTPA when the TPEF technique is employed to compare nonlinearities from a colloid (m-CNDs) and a molecular solution (R6G). Details of the concentration calculations are presented in the Supplementary Information. The obtained concentrations are 9.85 × 1015, 2.46 × 1015, and 1.26 × 1015 particles/mL for the m-CNDs concentrations of 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5 mg mL−1, respectively.

The TPEF spectra obtained from m-CNDs at different concentrations and excited at the wavelength range 680 – 760 nm are shown in Fig. 6(a-c). These TPEF spectra are broader compared with the spectra observed for one-photon excitation (see Fig. 5), showing three main peaks at 445, 490 m and 530 nm whose relative intensity is determined by the two-photon excitation wavelength; these features are clearly distinguishable in m-CNDs of higher concentration (1.0 and 1.5 mg mL−1) in which the TPEF signal is more intense. The presence of three peaks in the TPEF spectra is explained by the simultaneous two-photon excitation of different emission centers. For instance, the two-photon excitation energy from a pair of infrared photons (2ħω) in the range 680 – 760 nm is equivalent to the one-photon excitation energy in the range 340 – 380 nm, which according to the excitation-emission maps of Fig. 5 would lead to an emission around 445 nm. This peak is clearly observed in the TPEF spectra and is denoted as blue oval shape in Fig. 6. Regarding the peaks at 490 and 530 nm, they are also present in the excitation-emission maps for a one-photon excitation in the range 400–430 nm, but it must be observed that in this range the photon energy is lower than the corresponding from a pair of infrared photons (2ħω) in the range 680 – 760 nm utilized to obtain the TPEF spectra. Thus, the emissions at 490 and 530 nm in Fig. 6 denote a blue-shifting of the TPA resonance with respect to one-photon resonance shown in Fig. 5. This type of blue-shifting is predicted in symmetric π-conjugated systems in which, according to Laporte’s rule [53], the one-photon and two-photon transitions are mutually exclusive. Thus, the emission at 490 and 530 nm can be assigned to edge and surface moieties of symmetric character. Fig. S2 illustrates the similarities of one TPEF spectra with the possible contributions of different emissive centers.

Fig. 7 presents the σTPA for m-CNDs of different concentrations. The TPEF spectra of the standard R6G tested under the same experimental are presented in the supplementary section (Fig. S3). It is observed that σTPA in the colloids of m-CNDs increased substantially at higher concentrations; hypothetically, such nonlinear enhancement can be originated from the aggregation of particles and improvement in the crystallinity while increasing the dopant level. Table 1 presents a comparison of representative carbon nanodots reported in the literature in which the TPA has been addressed. The maximum σTPA value in our samples was 19.7 × 103 GM for m-CNDs at 1.5 mg mL−1 upon 730 nm of excitation. This σTPA is 3-fold smaller than the largest values observed in N-rich CNDs [3], somewhat smaller than other metal doped CDs such as S, Se metal co-doped CNDs [21], buy it significantly surpasses other metal containing carbon nanodots such as in the case of Ru-doped CNDs [20] and other variety of reported CNDs [54–57]. We also can see that the maximum nonlinearities here measured resulted similar to those of polymer-surface passivated CNDs [16] or porphyrin-conjugated CNDS [15]. Thus, the Na-doping resulted in a suitable strategy to enhance the nonlinearities in CNDs. The high PL promoted by TPA in the m-CNDs might result useful for imaging of cells. Fig. S4 shows that cells treated with CNDS and m-CNDS exhibit acceptable viability.

To summarize, m-CNDs were fabricated via a green and one-step solid-phase flux technique with U and CA as carbon source and sodium as a metal dopant agent. m-CNDs exhibited tunable PL emission across the entire visible spectrum with enhanced PLQY and σTPA compared with their pristine counterpart. The obtained m-CNDs in colloids of different concentration varied in size due aggregation effects, consequently, the augmentation effect led also to enhanced nonlinearities per particle. i.e., the two-photon absorption cross section increased with the size of the carbon nanodot. The maximum σTPA resulted in the order of 104 GM, which is very competitive with other representative CNDs previously reported. The excellent photophysical properties and concentration-dependent behavior observed with the Na doping offer a path to tune the emission wavelengths in carbon nanodots.

Velusamy Jayaramakrishnan: Investigation (general optical studies, linear and nonlinear), Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Gabriel Ramos-Ortiz: Conceptualization, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Resources, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: G. Ramos-Ortiz acknowledges economical support from CONACyT (Grant 215708). We thank Sandeep Surendra Panikar for the assistance in cell viability assay.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127993.
